title: Quiz: Template Matching Optimization
description: Test your understanding of pattern-based circuit optimization techniques

questions.question: What are the essential components of an optimization template?

options.text: A unitary matrix and its decomposition
Just the gate count before and after

questions.explanation: A template requires a pattern (circuit fragment to find), a replacement (equivalent but optimized circuit), and optionally constraints for valid application (e.g., parameter relationships).

questions.question: The pattern H-X-H can be replaced with which single gate?
questions.explanation: H-X-H = Z. This is because Hadamard conjugates Pauli operators: HXH = Z, HZH = X, and HYH = -Y. This is a fundamental identity in quantum computing.

questions.question: CNOT(a,b)-CNOT(b,a)-CNOT(a,b) is equivalent to:
questions.explanation: Three CNOTs with alternating control/target implement a SWAP gate. This is the standard decomposition: SWAP = CNOT(a,b)-CNOT(b,a)-CNOT(a,b). The template can recognize this and replace with SWAP if beneficial.

questions.question: Template matching can only find patterns where gates are directly adjacent in the circuit.
questions.explanation: While simple template matching uses sequential patterns, graph-based (DAG) template matching can find patterns across non-adjacent gates by considering the dependency structure of the circuit.

questions.question: Why might a template have zero or negative cost reduction?

options.text: To reorder gates in a way that enables other optimizations

questions.explanation: Some templates reorder or expand gates to enable subsequent optimizations. For example, commuting Z past CNOT (zero cost) may allow Z gates to merge with others. Such enabling transformations are valuable in multi-pass optimization.

questions.question: T-T simplifies to which gate?
questions.explanation: T applies a phase of pi/4, so T-T applies pi/2 phase, which is the S gate. Similarly, S-S = Z (pi phase) and T-T-T-T = Z.
